name: Golang
scope: A Go developer with INTERMEDIATE proficiency levels, with 3-6 years of experience, has solid command of Go’s core syntax (slices, maps, structs, interfaces, pointers), concurrency (goroutines, channels, worker pools), and error handling (custom errors, error wrapping). They structure projects with Go modules/packages, follow Go idioms, and leverage design patterns like factory, decorator, or adapter. They write unit/integration tests using the `testing` package, incorporate mocking frameworks, and track coverage metrics with linting tools. They handle day-to-day coding tasks like debugging concurrency issues, managing semantic versioning conflicts, optimizing performance, and preventing race conditions or deadlocks. They use `go mod` for dependency management, create reproducible builds, and integrate with Docker and CI/CD pipelines. They design REST/gRPC services (often using `gorilla/mux`), manage API versioning, and implement basic microservice patterns (pub-sub, message queues). They identify performance bottlenecks using profiling and benchmarking tools (`pprof`, `go test -bench`) and apply caching and concurrency best practices. They handle security concerns (secret management, injection prevention), implement logging frameworks (`zap`, `logrus`), and integrate authentication (JWT, OAuth2). They stay aware of newer features (generics) and maintain familiarity with popular libraries (e.g., `sqlx`, `gorm`) while refining architectural thinking.

name: Redis
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Redis as a competency should be able to design and maintain a coherent key-space with naming, TTL and eviction strategy, select and combine all core data types (String, List, Hash, Set, Sorted Set, HyperLogLog, Bitmap, Stream, Geo) while using Lua, MULTI/EXEC, WATCH and PIPELINE for atomic, batched, optimistic workflows and accurately sizing memory. They configure RDB, AOF and hybrid persistence, tune appendfsync and rewrite thresholds, automate backup, restore, point-in-time recovery and execute zero-data-loss upgrades or migrations. They deploy and run Sentinel with correct quorum, down-after-milliseconds and failover-timeout, analyse pub/sub channels to avoid split-brain and perform manual or automatic role switches. They provision and reshard Redis Cluster, assign hash-slots, grow or shrink nodes, determine shard count and replication factor and tune client smart-routing and slot cache refresh. They benchmark with redis-benchmark or k6, inspect INFO, SLOWLOG, MONITOR, LATENCY DOCTOR, then adjust maxmemory, active-defrag, hz, io-threads, client-output-buffers and TCP settings to hit p-99 latency targets. They integrate Prometheus or StatsD exporters, create Grafana dashboards, use RedisInsight or CLI-perf tools, and alert on replication offset, evictions, rejected connections and command distribution. They harden security through AUTH, ACLs, SSL/TLS, protected-mode, command renaming, disabling CONFIG, secret rotation and least-privilege role mapping. They embed Redis in micro-services for read-through/write-through/lazy-invalidate caching, implement distributed locks, rate limiting, leader election, task queues, Pub/Sub and Streams consumer groups with back-pressure and fencing tokens, and tune pools, retries and circuit breakers in Java, .NET, Python, Go, NodeJS and Rust clients. They execute rolling restarts, CONFIG REWRITE, online memory rescale, patch/minor upgrades via Terraform, Ansible, Helm or CloudFormation modules in Docker/Kubernetes pipelines and maintain comprehensive runbooks. They troubleshoot latency spikes, failovers and memory leaks by correlating client telemetry, server logs and OS metrics, replicate issues with custom redis-server configs and produce post-mortems and capacity plans. Finally, they evaluate and integrate modules such as Bloom, JSON, TimeSeries, Search and Graph, understand single-threaded execution, big-O per command, replication lag and consistency windows, and articulate trade-offs versus alternative stores.
long_scope:
INTERMEDIATE-level Redis Competency Scope (≈ 3-5 years focused, production-grade experience)

1. Core Data-Model & Command Mastery  
   • Designs and maintains a logically consistent key-space, naming standards, and TTL strategy that aligns with domain entities and SLAs.  
   • Selects, combines, and tunes all built-in data structures (String, List, Hash, Set, Sorted Set, HyperLogLog, Bitmap, Stream, Geo) to meet functional and non-functional requirements.  
   • Applies Lua scripting, transactions (MULTI/EXEC), optimistic locking (WATCH), and server-side batching (PIPELINE) to guarantee atomicity, reduce round-trips, and control concurrency under moderate contention.  
   • Calculates memory footprint of chosen encodings, predicts expiry behaviour, and calibrates eviction policies to sustain cache hit-rates and protect critical keys.  

2. Persistence & Durability Strategy  
   • Configures and operates both RDB snapshots and AOF with hybrid policies, choosing append-fsync and rewrite thresholds that balance durability latency and I/O overhead.  
   • Automates backup, verifies restore, and performs point-in-time recovery for single-node or clustered deployments without SLA breach.  
   • Executes zero-data-loss upgrade or migration between minor versions using replica promotion, controlled failover, or temp slaves.  

3. High Availability & Automated Failover  
   • Deploys and maintains a Sentinel-based topology (quorum sizing, down-after-milliseconds, failover-timeout) for single-primary, multi-replica configurations across availability zones.  
   • Diagnoses split-brain or false positives by analysing Sentinel pub/sub channels and adjusting network- and disk-I/O-related parameters.  
   • Performs controlled role switches for maintenance tasks, validating application reconnection behaviour and preventing dual-primary scenarios.  

4. Clustered Sharding & Horizontal Scale  
   • Provisions a Redis Cluster, assigns hash-slots, and expands or shrinks nodes with resharding while sustaining predictable latency.  
   • Determines shard count based on memory growth projections, replication factor, and topology (rack-aware, zone-aware).  
   • Tunes client libraries for smart-routing, slot cache refresh, and multi-key operation fall-back.  

5. Performance & Memory Optimisation  
   • Benchmarks with redis-benchmark or k6, profiling latency and throughput under target workloads, then correlates with CPU, memory allocator, and network statistics.  
   • Interprets OUTPUT from INFO, SLOWLOG, MONITOR, and LATENCY DOCTOR to isolate hot keys, blocking commands, or fragmentation above safe thresholds.  
   • Adjusts maxmemory, active-defrag, hz, io-threads, client-output-buffer limits, and TCP settings to meet p-99 latency budgets.  

6. Observability & Operational Tooling  
   • Integrates Redis exporters with Prometheus or StatsD, curates Grafana dashboards covering replication offset, rejected connections, evicted keys, and command distribution.  
   • Installs and uses RedisInsight, KeyDB-Browser, or CLI-perf tools for memory analysis and real-time command tracing.  
   • Implements alerting rules that prevent both false positives and SLA-impacting misses (e.g. RTO > X ms, RPO > Y sec, evictions > Z/sec).  

7. Security, Governance & Compliance  
   • Enforces AUTH, ACLs, SSL/TLS, and protected-mode, rotating credentials through CI/CD secrets stores.  
   • Differentiates ACL categories per service role, auditing command access with minimal privilege.  
   • Documents and automates CIS-aligned hardening (bind addresses, renaming sensitive commands, disabling CONFIG on shared hosts).  

8. Application Integration Patterns  
   • Embeds Redis within micro-services for read-through, write-through, and lazy-invalidate caching, ensuring consistency with backing stores.  
   • Implements Distributed Locks, Rate Limiting, Leader Election, and Task Queues with proper timeouts, fencing tokens, or logical clocks to avoid dead-locks and stale locks.  
   • Utilises Pub/Sub and Streams for event fan-out and durable log processing, configuring consumer-group offsets, pending entries, and back-pressure handling.  
   • Tunes connection pools, retry logic, and circuit breakers in client libraries (Java, .NET, Python, Go, NodeJS, Rust) for efficient multiplexing and failure resilience.  

9. Maintenance & Lifecycle Management  
   • Executes rolling restarts, online config rewrites, and safe memory rescale operations without breaching SLOs.  
   • Applies patch-level security updates, minor version upgrades, deprecates legacy commands, and validates behaviour changes via staging canaries.  
   • Crafts Infrastructure-as-Code (Terraform/Ansible/Helm/CloudFormation) modules to provision, configure, and destroy Redis resources across cloud or on-prem environments.  

10. Troubleshooting & Root-Cause Analysis  
   • Triages latency spikes, failovers, or memory leaks by correlating client telemetry, server logs, and underlying OS metrics.  
   • Recreates edge cases in isolated environments using redis-server with custom config overrides and reproducer scripts.  
   • Produces incident post-mortems that include timeline, trigger, blast radius, corrective actions, and preventive enhancements.  

11. Awareness of Ecosystem & Modules (User-Level)  
   • Evaluates and integrates plug-and-play modules (Bloom, JSON, TimeSeries, Search, Graph) to extend capability where fit.  
   • Assesses licensing, memory, and performance impact before enabling or disabling module functionality, and ensures compatibility during upgrades.  

12. Soft Skills & Collaboration  
   • Communicates Redis operational constraints to developers, SREs, and security teams, driving best-practice adoption.  
   • Contributes to internal knowledge bases, runbooks, and design reviews, providing reasoned trade-offs among cost, latency, durability, and complexity.  
   • Mentors junior engineers on fundamental command usage and production hygiene while deferring deeper internals to senior experts when necessary.  

Deliverables & Outcomes Expected at This Level  
   – Production environments with ≤ 1 % cache miss on critical paths, ≤ 100 ms p-99 latency, and documented RPO/RTO boundaries.  
   – Automated pipelines for provisioning, backup, and deploy with unit/integration tests covering Lua scripts and cluster failover scenarios.  
   – Dashboards, alerts, and runbooks achieving operational readiness sign-off from SRE/DevOps peers.  
   – Post-incident reports and capacity plans that influence quarterly engineering roadmaps.  

Tools a Practitioner Is Comfortable With  
   redis-cli, redis-benchmark, redis-check-aof, redis-check-rdb, RedisInsight, Prometheus exporter, Grafana, Terraform modules, Ansible playbooks, Sentinel CLI, Cluster util scripts (redis-trib or redis-cli ‑-cluster), CI/CD platforms, container orchestrators (Docker/Kubernetes), version-control workflows (Git).  

Conceptual Depth  
   • Solid, scenario-driven understanding of replication lag, consistency windows, single-threaded execution implications, event loop mechanics, and network round-trip costs.  
   • Practical comprehension of big-O complexity per command and its impact on large key/element counts.  
   • Clear picture of when to choose Redis versus alternate stores, articulating trade-offs in terms of persistence, query expressiveness, and scaling models.
